title: Wyoming
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Wyoming, sentencing courts have wide discretion to vary the degree of indeterminacy in a sentence, with minimums allowed to range up to 90 percent of the maximum. Good time credits can achieve a one-third reduction (15 days per month) in a mandatory release date and advance parole eligibility.¹

title: Wyoming
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
In 2019, 39 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 61 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 34 percent.

title: Wyoming
text:
Demographics

Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 74 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 88 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.

title: Wyoming
text:
Offense Types
In 2019, 52 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 48 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (30 percent) offenses.

title: Wyoming
text:
Sentence Lengths
In 2019, 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 5 and 9.9 years.

title: Wyoming
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (90 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 90 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Wyoming
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Utah
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Utah, sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.¹

text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (11 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 11 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Tennessee
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Tennessee, sentences under two years are released to probation automatically upon eligibility. There is a statutory staggered percentage formula (generally 30–60 percent of the maximum sentence) for parole-release eligibility, differentiated by sentencing “range” based on prior convictions. Starting in 2021, there is a growing list of 100-percent offenses.¹

title: Tennessee
text:

title: Tennessee
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (60 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 60 for those whose most serious offense is violent, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in Tennesee, the percentage of the max. sentence that corresponds to the min. sentence depends on previous sentences.Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, all cases were assumed to have previous sentences. Consequently, the rules-based estimated min. sentence was replaced by 60 percent of the max. sentence. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Tennessee
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: South Carolina
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In South Carolina, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release at 25 or 33 percent of the maximum sentence with the possibility of somewhat earlier eligibility through credit earnings of 20 days per month. “No parole” crimes are not eligible for parole until they have served 85 percent of the judicial sentence, with three days of credit per month.¹

title: South Carolina
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
In 2019, 27 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 73 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 9 percent.

title: South Carolina
text:
Demographics

Race and EthnicitySexAge

In 2019, 93 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.

title: South Carolina
text:
Offense Types
In 2019, 38 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 62 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (38 percent) offenses.

title: South Carolina
text:
Sentence Lengths
In 2019, 46 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: South Carolina
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (25 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Third, in South Carolina there is no parole for people with two or more violent offenses. Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2020, rules-based estimated PEY was removed for every one with a violent offense. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Pennsylvania
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Pennsylvania judges have discretion to set the durations of minimum terms to parole-release eligibility, which can be any length up to 50 percent of the maximum.¹

text:

text:

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues and the state’s code.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of when the most serious offense is non-violent and 25 yearsfor people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was shorter. Third, in Pennsylvania, people with previous terms have additional restrictions. Because data on previous terms was not published for 2018 and 2019, they were applied to everyoned. Thus, everyone with a max. sentence between 7 and 24 years assigned a min. sentence of 7 years and everyone with a max. sentence of 25 or more years was assigned a min. sentence of 25, if those minimums were greater than the previouslyassigned minimum. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Pennsylvania
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: North Dakota
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In North Dakota, most people who are incarcerated are eligible for discretionary parole release soon after they enter prison. The parole board has broad discretion to set minimum terms to parole release consideration for each prisoner individually. Violent offenses are subject to an 85-percent eligibility rule.¹

title: North Dakota
text:

From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 51 percent.

title: North Dakota

In 2019, 29 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 71 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drug (37 percent) offenses.

title: North Dakota
text:
Sentence Lengths
In 2019, 44 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 and 4.9 years.

title: North Dakota
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (0 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: New York
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In New York, most people who are incarcerated are serving “determinate” sentences, but a substantial number are serving “indeterminate” sentences that receive a judicial minimum and maximum, with no statutory constraint on the ratio between them. People serving indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole “at any time” after the expiration of their minimum sentences.¹

title: New York
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 1 year when the most serious offense is non-violent and for people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was shorter. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 50 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: New Hampshire
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Nebraska
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Nebraska, prison sentences with maximum terms of four years or less have no parole release eligibility. Maximum terms of 20 years or more almost always include parole-release eligibility, with reductions for good time credits; this prison population becomes eligible at the 50-percent mark of the judicial maximum sentence or earlier.¹

title: Missouri
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Missouri, minimum terms to parole-release eligibility for most people who are incarcerated are established by regulations by the parole board, with a range of 15–33 percent of the maximum sentence. By statute, dangerous offenses serve 85 percent of the maximum to be eligible, and repeated commitments to prison for other serious offenses result in increasing minimums of 40, 50, and 70 percent.¹

title: Missouri
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Michigan
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Michigan, by statute, fixed maximum terms must be included in most judicial sentences. Minimum terms can be as short as the judge elects but may not exceed two-thirds of the maximum sentence. There is no system of good time, earned time, or similar credits that may be accumulated by people who are incarcerated as a discount.¹

title: Michigan
text:
Sentence Lengths

title: Michigan
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (67 percent for those who’s most serious offense is non-violent, and 67 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate. Because of this, data reported for Michigan is from 2018, except for the 2023 estimate.Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Kansas
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Generally, prisoners convicted of lower- severity felonies may earn 20-percent good-time reductions of their maximum terms while prisoners convicted of higher-severity felonies can earn 15 percent.¹

title: Kansas
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Indiana
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
With no parole release, reductions in time served are only through good time credit, which depends on prisoners’ classification into one of several tiers. Day for day credit is the maximum tier for a 50% discount but educational credit may further increase the discount.¹

title: Georgia
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Georgia, people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences, but no earlier than after 9 months and no later than after 7 years.¹

title: Georgia
text:
In Prison Past Parole Eligibility
In 2019, 60 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 40 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019.





From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 39 percent.

title: Georgia
text:
Demographics

Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 59 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 94 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male.






In 2019, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.

title: Georgia
text:
Offense Types
In 2019, 70 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 30 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (19 percent), robbery (19 percent), and aggravated or simple assault (19 percent) offenses.

title: Georgia
text:
Sentence Lengths
In 2019, 53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: Georgia
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 9 months when the most serious offense is non-violent and 7 yearsfor people with offenses of more than 21 years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was longer. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Georgia
text:
Endnotes
¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Matthew Jacobs, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Georgia (Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, July 2022), https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-07/georgia_doi_report_7_7_22.pdf.² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., “National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, “National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022” (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.Georgia Department of Corrections, Daily Population by Facility Calendar Year 2023 (Atlanta, GA: Georgia Department of Corrections, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://gdc.georgia.gov/document/annual-statistical-reports/daily-population-facility-cy-2023/download.
Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners.

title: Georgia
text:
Prison Population Trends
From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 17 percent, changing from 56,432 in 2010 to 47,010 in 2022.

title: Georgia
text:
Demographics

Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 60 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 93 percent of people in prison were male.






In 2019, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old.

title: Georgia
text:
Offense Types
In 2019, 15 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault offenses.

title: Georgia
text:
Sentence Lengths
In 2019, 42 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years.

title: Georgia
text:
Prison Release Trends
From 2010 to 2020, the number people released from prison increased 9 percent, dropping from 16,210 in 2010 to 17,699 in 2020.





In 2020, 82 percent of parole-eligible people released in Georgia were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 12 percent from 2010.

title: Georgia
text:
Conditional Releases
Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 42 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases.

title: Georgia
text:
Demographics

Race and EthnicitySexAge


In 2019, 53 percent of people released from prison were Black, non-Hispanic.






In 2019, 88 percent of people released from prison were male.






In 2019, 37 percent of people released from prison were 25-34 years.

title: Georgia
text:
Offense Types
In 2019, 28 percent of people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses.

title: Georgia
text:
Disparities in Parole Release

Race and EthnicitySex




In 2019, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Georgia. 

In 2019, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.2 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Georgia.Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity.




In 2019, males  were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.3 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in Georgia.

In 2019, Black people released spent on average 0.9 more years in prison, and Hispanic people released spent on average 0.8 more years in prison compared to White people.




The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where Hispanic people spent on average 1.7 more years in prison compared to White people.

The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among murder or nonnegligent manslaughter offenses, where males spent on average 2.5 more years in prison compared to females.

title: Colorado
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For most felonies in Colorado, people who are incarcerated become eligible for discretionary parole release after they have served 50 percent of the sentence imposed by the court. This eligibility formula assumes that a person in prison has earned all available good time credits of 15 days per month.¹

title: Colorado
text:
Sentence Lengths
In 2019, 53 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 and 4.9 years.

title: Colorado
text:
Estimation Methodology
Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state’s parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (50 percent). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 2 years, only when the most serious offense might be one with special eligibility restrictions. is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state’s department of corrections’ report (2023).⁴

title: Colorado
text:
Notes on Disparities
Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes
In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in criminal justice outcomes—including incarceration rates, parole eligibility, and time served—across race, ethnicity, and sex.
The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility.
Metrics Displayed in Visualizations
Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex:

RRI of Parole Release: This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole.
Average Time Past Parole Eligibility: For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups.
Average Total Time Served: We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities.

Data Considerations Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology.
Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states:

Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations.
Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals.

Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation
Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as “Other race(s), non-Hispanic” because the small numbers in this group limit the ability to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis.

title: Alabama
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
In Alabama, sentences of five years or less have no minimum term to parole-release eligibility. Sentences of more than five and up to 10 years have an initial parole consideration approximately 18 months prior to the minimum release date. Sentences of more than 10 and up to 15 years have an initial parole consideration 30 months prior to the minimum release date.¹

All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate. title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

title: National Snapshot
text: [This page uses 2019 data for now. Will be replaced with 2023 data when available.] While the most recent available data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) is from 2020, we’ve projected the numbers for 2023 to offer a more current view of how many people are in prison beyond their parole eligibility and how this impacts prison populations. These projections are conservative estimates for several reasons. Primarily, we did not fully account for ‘good time’ credits—time that should be subtracted from an someone’s sentence, thereby reducing the time until parole eligibility. Additionally, there are other factors contributing to the conservative nature of these estimates that were not fully captured in our models. As a result, the actual number of people held beyond their eligible parole dates is likely higher than our data suggests, indicating a more significant issue than our conservative estimates reflect.

title: California
text:
How Parole Eligibility Is Determined
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
California’s prison system is dominated by determinate sentences and parolable sentences with long minimum terms. Prison sentences are generally expressed as “determinate terms” that operate as judicial maximum sentences. Most determinate sentences carry no parole-release eligibility but are reducible by conduct-based credits of one kind or another. Changes in the law since 2014 have expanded the classes of prison sentences that carry parole-release eligibility, including some “determinate” sentences for nonviolent crimes.¹
